title: Writing - Part 1: Cybersecurity
description: Your class has listened to a talk on cybersecurity and the growing threats that individuals, organisations and governments face in an increasingly interconnected digital world.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the challenges in your notes. You should explain which challenge you consider most critical for society to address, giving reasons for your opinion.

challenges: The human factor and social engineering attacks
opinions: Cyber attacks targeting power grids, water treatment facilities, financial systems and healthcare networks have the potential to cause widespread harm on a societal scale, making the protection of critical infrastructure a matter of national security.

The vast quantities of personal data collected by governments, corporations and digital platforms create systemic vulnerabilities that, if exploited, can expose millions of individuals to identity theft, financial fraud and surveillance.

Research consistently shows that the majority of successful cyber attacks exploit human behaviour rather than technical vulnerabilities, through phishing, credential theft and social engineering tactics that no firewall can fully prevent.

structure.introduction: Introduce the cybersecurity landscape and identify the two challenges your essay will address.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Analyse the first challenge, explaining its mechanisms and the scale of potential harm.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the second challenge and argue why you believe it is the most critical priority for society.
structure.conclusion: Bring your argument to a close and reaffirm your view of the most pressing challenge.

content.modelAnswer:
The digitalisation of virtually every domain of modern life has created extraordinary opportunities but has simultaneously exposed individuals, organisations and states to an expanding range of cybersecurity threats. Among the most significant are attacks on critical national infrastructure and the exploitation of individual human behaviour through social engineering — two challenges that are distinct in nature but deeply interconnected in practice.

The targeting of critical infrastructure represents perhaps the most immediately alarming dimension of the cybersecurity threat. State-sponsored and criminal actors have demonstrated both the capability and the willingness to attack power grids, water treatment systems, hospitals and financial networks with potentially catastrophic consequences. The 2021 ransomware attack on a major American fuel pipeline, which caused fuel shortages across several states, offered a sobering illustration of how digital vulnerabilities translate into tangible real-world disruption. As these systems become more connected, the attack surface available to hostile actors expands accordingly.

Nevertheless, I would argue that the human factor constitutes the most critical challenge, precisely because it cannot be resolved through technological investment alone. Studies consistently attribute the majority of successful breaches to phishing emails, credential theft and psychological manipulation rather than to the exploitation of unpatched software vulnerabilities. Even the most sophisticated technical defences are rendered ineffective if the individuals operating within those systems can be deceived into inadvertently granting access. Addressing this challenge requires sustained investment in digital literacy, behavioural training and organisational security culture — areas that remain chronically underfunded relative to technical infrastructure.

In conclusion, while protecting critical infrastructure demands urgent attention, embedding genuine security awareness across society is the more fundamental and enduring challenge.
